data.title: DoorDash, Uber rejected in bid to block NYC tipping law
data.description:
DoorDash (DASH) and Uber Technologies (UBER) were unsuccessful in their attempt to block the implementation of a New York City law that requires a tipping option be given to customers at checkout.

"Plaintiffs have not made the required showing of a likelihood of success on the merits, irreparable harm, or that the balance of equities and public interest favor the extraordinary remedy of preliminary relief," said New York US District Judge George Daniels in his ruling.

Beginning next week, a new NYC law will require delivery apps to prompt customers to include a tip of at least 10% to ensure minimum earnings for delivery drivers after a new minimum wage law went into effect in 2023.

Delivery companies like Uber (UBER) and DoorDash (DASH) argue that a minimum gratuity forces customers to give a tip when one is neither earned nor necessary, while others claim that if a customer doesn’t leave a large enough tip at the beginning of the transaction, drivers won’t take the order.

“Forcing platforms to solicit a tip before checkout at a time when New Yorkers are sick of tipping culture and facing a growing affordability crisis is bad policy—plain and simple,” a DoorDash representative said to the New York Post.

Judge Daniels decision follows an allegation from the New York City Department of Consumer and Worker Protection ((DWCP)) that Uber Eats (UBER) and DoorDash (DASH) designed their platform to discourage tipping.

“Forcing people to tip may as well be a tax. It should be up to consumers, not politicians, whether they want to tip more in New York after already paying for a billion-dollar raise for workers," DoorDash’s head of North American policy, John Horton, said in a statement to Seeking Alpha.

According to a survey by WalletHub, nearly 90% of respondents believe tipping culture has “gotten out of control,” while 30% tip less when prompted for a suggested tip.

data.title: Smart eyewear firm Solos sues Meta, EssilorLuxottica for patent infringement: Bloomberg
data.description:
Meta Platforms Inc. (META) and its smart glasses partner EssilorLuxottica SA are being sued by Solos Technology Ltd., a smaller tech eyewear company that is alleging patent infringement, Bloomberg reported on Friday.

The report said Solos filed the complaint in a federal Massachusetts court and is seeking "multiple billions of dollars" in damages and an injunction.

The infringement filing mostly focuses on the Ray-Ban Meta Wayfarer Gen 1 but says, “Defendants’ later releases are derivative of the Gen 1 platform and continue to implement Solos’ patented technologies, resulting in ongoing and continuous infringement,” the report said.

Solos also claims that Oakley employees were introduced to its preliminary glasses technology as early as 2015, with a former senior executive later receiving a commercial pair for testing purposes in 2019.

Similarly, the complaint alleges that EssilorLuxottica met with Solos personnel throughout 2017 and gained knowledge of the company’s concepts and roadmap.

As for Meta, Solos alleges that MIT Sloan Fellow Priyanka Shekar published a 2021 research study titled “Audio Wearable Product Strategy: Expanding User Experience for Solos Smart Glasses” and then brought that knowledge to Meta when she joined the company as a product manager. Her study cited Solos’ patents as one of the company’s strengths.

“By the time Meta jointly commercialized smart-glasses products in or around 2021 with EssilorLuxottica, both sides had accumulated years of direct, senior-level, and increasingly detailed knowledge of Solos’ smart-glasses technology,” the complaint said.

data.title: Novo Nordisk Wegovy pill uptake negatively impacts syringe makers
data.description:
• Becton, Dickinson (BDX) and West Pharmaceutical Services (WST), two of the largest manufacturers of syringes, are down Friday following strong initial prescription figures for Novo Nordisk's (NVO) oral Wegovy (semaglutide) pill for weight loss. 
  • The decline is due to the perception that oral GLP-1s could cannibalize GLP-1s that are administered via injection.
  • BD is off ~2%, while West Pharmaceutical is down ~5%.
  • JP Morgan's Robbie Marcus, who has a neutral rating on BD, noted that its syringe exposure for GLP-1s is less than 2% of sales. "While strength in oral GLP-1s could reduce the size of the total opportunity versus a world with injectables alone, we still expect injectables will have an important role in the treatment paradigm with orals mostly used for maintenance."

data.title: Tech Voices: Nvidia China chip sales, Amazon layoffs, TikTok
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact the technology sector.

  • Chinese officials have informed some of China's leading tech companies that they can prepare to place orders for Nvidia's  (NVDA) H200 AI chips, which the U.S. government recently approved for shipment to that country.

According to Bloomberg, Beijing is allowing Alibaba (BABA), Tencent (TCEHY) (TCTZF), and ByteDance (DNCE) to begin discussing order details, but will also encourage them to buy domestically produced chips as a condition for final approval.

  • Amazon (AMZN) plans to lay off around 14,000 workers beginning next week as part of a larger plan to trim its workforce by approximately 30,000 people.

The tech giant eliminated roughly 14,000 jobs last year during its first round of layoffs. The second round, which could begin as early as Tuesday, is expected to impact a similar number of jobs, according to Reuters, which cited people close to the matter.

Divisions and units slated for cuts include retail, People Experience and Technology, Amazon Web Services, and Prime Video, Reuters added.

  • President Trump praised the establishment of a new, American-run joint venture to oversee TikTok's U.S. operations.

"I am so happy to have helped in saving TikTok! It will now be owned by a group of Great American Patriots and Investors, the Biggest in the World, and will be an important Voice," said Trump in a post late Thursday on Truth Social.

"Along with other factors, it was responsible for my doing so well with the Youth Vote in the 2024 Presidential Election. I only hope that long into the future I will be remembered by those who use and love TikTok," Trump continued.

"I would also like to thank President Xi, of China, for working with us and, ultimately, approving the Deal. He could have gone the other way, but didn’t, and is appreciated for his decision," Trump added.

data.title: Eastern Bankshares signals 3–5% loan growth and focuses on organic expansion while targeting 12% CET1 ratio
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Eastern Bankshares, Inc. (EBC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Robert Rivers, Executive Chairman of the Board, highlighted that "2025 was a terrific year for Eastern, highlighted by a 62% increase in operating earnings, strong organic loan growth and a record level of wealth assets under management." He emphasized the merger with HarborOne as a milestone, strengthening the presence in key markets and providing entry into Rhode Island. Rivers stated, "As a result, we will not pursue any acquisitions as we are completely focused on organic growth and returning capital to our shareholders for the foreseeable future."
  • CEO Denis Sheahan echoed the focus on organic growth, stating "We have plenty of opportunities to organically grow the company's earnings and enhance profitability, and that is our focus." Sheahan noted the company's strategy to allocate capital towards organic growth and returning excess capital through share repurchases and dividends.
  • Sheahan underscored successful integration of new talent and the commercial lending platform, noting "We originated $2.5 billion of commercial loans in 2025... pipelines remain solid heading into 2026."
  • Sheahan also reported that wealth assets reached a record high of $10.1 billion at year-end, with $9.6 billion in assets under management.
  • CFO R. Rosato stated, "Q4 marked a strong finish to the year as we reported net income of $99.5 million or $0.46 per diluted share... Operating earnings of $94.7 million increased 28% linked quarter. On a per diluted share basis, operating earnings increased 19% to $0.44."

Outlook

  • The company provided a full year 2026 outlook, projecting loan growth of 3% to 5% and deposit growth of 1% to 2%. Net interest income is anticipated to be in the range of $1.20 billion to $1.50 billion with a full year FTE margin of 3.65% to 3.75%.
  • Provision expense for 2026 is expected to be $30 million to $40 million. Operating noninterest income is expected between $190 million and $200 million, and operating noninterest expense should be in the range of $655 million to $675 million.
  • The company intends to manage its CET1 ratio towards 12% and continue significant capital return through share repurchases and dividends. Management reiterated a strategic stance of not pursuing acquisitions and focusing on organic growth.

Financial Results

  • Net income for Q4 was $99.5 million, or $0.46 per diluted share. Operating earnings reached $94.7 million.
  • Operating ROA for the fourth quarter was 130 basis points, while return on average tangible common equity was 13.8%.
  • Net interest income was $237.4 million. The margin was 3.61% for the quarter, up from 3.47% in Q3.
  • Noninterest income for Q4 was $46.1 million, with mortgage banking income increasing to $3 million. Investment advisory fees rose to $18.6 million, and interest rate swap income reached $1.4 million.
  • Noninterest expense totaled $189.4 million, with nonoperating expenses of $33.4 million primarily due to merger-related costs and a $3.5 million lease impairment.
  • Deposits at period-end totaled $25.5 billion, an increase reflecting the HarborOne addition. Loans increased $4.7 billion linked quarter, primarily from the merger.
  • Allowance for loan losses was $332 million, or 144 basis points of total loans, up from $233 million at the end of Q3.

Q&A

  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde Group, LLC, Research Division, asked about margin trajectory. Rosato responded that "Our margin forecast does ramp up each -- marginally each quarter, accelerates a little bit in the back half of the year."
  • Strickland also inquired about pipeline mix for loan growth. Sheahan explained the pipeline is "about a little bit more than 50% CRE, between CRE and community development lending and the other, say, 45% is C&I."
  • Damon Del Monte, KBW, asked about provision outlook. Rosato stated, "The guidance is similar to what we gave last year. And then in '25, we outperformed the guidance. Our thoughts are generally the same."
  • Gregory Zingone, Piper Sandler, sought a breakdown of AUM growth. Sheahan replied, "We had about $200 million of net flows in the fourth quarter, really strong, good momentum building." Zingone also asked about nonperforming credits, and Rosato clarified, "They are not located in downtown Boston. The NPLs were completely driven by HarborOne... There's no surprises in there whatsoever to us."
  • Laura Havener Hunsicker, Seaport Research, questioned the lease impairment and nonperforming loans. Rosato confirmed it was a one-time event and discussed the timeline for resolving nonperforming loans, stating, "I would expect similar experience here, maybe even a little faster" compared to previous integrations.
  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen, asked about fee income and capital management. Rosato said fee income from HarborOne's mortgage business will "probably be 8% to 10% of total fee income" and discussed ongoing plans to manage the CET1 ratio towards 12%.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for details on margin trajectory, provision guidance, nonperforming loans, and capital deployment, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone, with a focus on clarity and future growth prospects.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone throughout, emphasizing organic growth, strong capital, and clear strategic priorities. Rosato and Sheahan repeatedly stressed their focus on organic expansion and returning capital.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts maintained a similar level of inquiry, while management's tone shifted from cautious optimism regarding M&A to a firmer stance against acquisitions and a stronger commitment to buybacks and organic growth, as highlighted by Sheahan: "We're not pursuing acquisitions. We're entirely focused on the growth of this company, the organic growth."

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The most significant change from Q3 is the definitive move away from M&A in favor of organic growth and share repurchases. The language regarding acquisitions became more definitive, with Sheahan stating they "will not pursue any acquisitions."
  • The capital return program accelerated, with $55.4 million in share repurchases in Q4, compared to a new repurchase authorization and only preparation for HarborOne integration in Q3.
  • The company realized notable increases in operating earnings, margins, and wealth assets, as well as a substantial rise in allowance for loan losses due to the HarborOne merger.
  • Guidance language became more specific regarding targets for loan and deposit growth, margin, and capital ratios.
  • Analyst focus remained on credit quality, margin trajectory, and capital management, but questions about M&A receded as management's stance became unequivocal.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified an increase in nonperforming loans and criticized/classified loans, primarily due to acquired HarborOne credits. Rosato noted, "Nonperforming loans increased as expected by $103 million linked quarter, mostly due to $94 million of loans acquired from HarborOne... Reserve levels remain strong as demonstrated by an allowance for loan losses of $332 million."
  • The company acknowledged slightly elevated deposit costs due to the HarborOne deposit base, but expects to manage these costs down over time.
  • Provision expense guidance for 2026 reflects a conservative stance but not underlying credit concerns.
  • The integration of HarborOne's mortgage operations and resolution of acquired nonperforming loans are ongoing focus areas.

Final Takeaway

Eastern Bankshares, Inc. closes 2025 with strong operating performance, highlighted by successful organic loan growth, record wealth management assets, and the completed HarborOne merger. Management has signaled a clear strategic pivot to prioritize organic growth and aggressive capital return, moving away from M&A activity. The company enters 2026 with a strong capital base, firm guidance on profitability and growth targets, and a commitment to returning meaningful value to shareholders through share repurchases and dividends while maintaining a clear focus on credit quality and operational integration.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Meta temporarily freezes teen access to AI characters to add more parental controls
data.description:
Meta Platforms Inc. (META) said Friday it is temporarily restricting teens around the world from accessing AI characters across its family of apps.

The company said that after hearing feedback from parents about wanting more data on their teen's interaction data with AI characters, it started building a new version to fulfill those requests.

Once ready, the new version of Meta's AI characters will include additional parental controls. 

Teens will still be able to access helpful information and educational opportunities through Meta’s AI assistant, with default, age-appropriate protections in place, the company said.

"Starting in the coming weeks, teens will no longer be able to access AI characters across our apps until the updated experience is ready. This will apply to anyone who has given us a teen birthday, as well as people who claim to be adults but who we suspect are teens based on our age prediction technology," the company said in an updated blog post.

data.title: Byline Bancorp signals $99M–$100M Q1 net interest income outlook as it targets crossing $10B in assets in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Byline Bancorp (BY) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Roberto Herencia, Executive Chairman and CEO, highlighted a 20% increase in the quarterly dividend, describing it as "a reflection of our strong financial performance and confidence in our ability to continue to deliver top quartile results in key profitability metrics." Herencia emphasized Byline's strategic position, calling it "the largest local community bank, the second largest local commercial bank and the largest, most stable platform for quality lenders to bring their books and grow their businesses."

  • Alberto Paracchini, President and Director, reported the full year net income at $130.1 million or $2.89 per diluted share on revenue of $446 million, noting a 9.7% year-on-year revenue increase and 17% growth in tangible book value per share. He stated, "Profitability was strong with pretax preparation ROA of 219 basis points ROA of 136 basis points and ROTCE of 13.5%."

  • Paracchini noted the closure of the First Security transaction, the integration of systems within a single quarter, and the company's preparations to cross the $10 billion asset threshold in 2026. He also reported a share repurchase of approximately 346,000 shares and a new repurchase program authorizing buybacks of up to 5% of outstanding shares.

  • Thomas J. Bell, CFO, stated, "We had record high net interest income of $101 million in Q4, up 1.4% from the prior quarter, primarily due to loan growth, lower rates paid on deposits and lower interest expense related to the sub debt payoff, partially offset by lower yields on loans and securities."

Outlook

  • The company expects to cross the $10 billion asset threshold in 2026 and is monitoring regulatory changes related to asset thresholds but will not slow growth in anticipation.

  • Bell provided net interest income guidance for Q1 2026: "This implies a net interest income range of $99 million to $100 million for the first quarter."

  • Paracchini reinforced the focus on organic growth and highlighted the growth in the commercial payments business, which launched in April, onboarded six customers, and added approximately $70 million in liability balances.

  • Management expects loan growth to continue in the mid-single digits into 2026 and projects gain on sale of loans at an average of $5.5 million per quarter, with lower expectations in Q1 due to seasonality.

Financial Results

  • Q4 net income was $34.5 million or $0.76 per diluted share on revenue of $117 million. Revenue increased 1.1% from the prior quarter and 12% year-on-year, driven by higher net interest income.

  • Loans grew 3% linked quarter to $7.5 billion, while deposits declined to $7.65 billion due to year-end balance sheet management. Origination activity was $323 million, with commercial and leasing business as main contributors.

  • Noninterest income was $15.7 million, with gain on sale of loans at $5.4 million. Expenses held at $60.4 million, and the efficiency ratio was 50.3%.

  • Credit costs for the quarter were $9.7 million, with net charge-offs of $6.7 million and a reserve build of $3 million. The allowance for credit losses stands at 1.45% of total loans.

  • Capital ratios increased, with CET1 at 12.33% and TCE to TA at 11.29%.

Q&A

  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Asked about areas of growth and efficiency. Paracchini cited excitement for the commercial payments business and Byline's ability to gain commercial banking market share in Chicago.

  • Race, Piper Sandler: Inquired about capital targets and returns. Paracchini explained, "we do carry a bit more capital to allow for that... it has allowed us to move very, very quickly... when opportunities come up in the market, and we like that."

  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Queried loan growth outlook. Paracchini said, "commercial, I would still say that, Damon. Real estate... is going to be a function of transaction activity."

  • Del Monte, KBW: Sought clarity on NII seasonality. Bell responded, "there's fewer days in the quarter. So that's one drag... But generally speaking, again, stable to growing throughout the year."

  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group: Asked about origination pace and NII guidance. Paracchini emphasized the challenge in predicting payoffs and said the mid-single-digit loan growth guidance remains solid.

  • Nosal, Hovde Group: On NII guidance, Bell noted, "at some point, you run out of room there to continue to drop deposit costs in a meaningful way."

  • Terence McEvoy, Stephens: Asked about the commercial payments team’s clients. Paracchini explained onboarding includes payroll processing companies and select fintechs, with a deliberate approach and onboarding process of 6 to 9 months.

  • Brian Martin, Janney Montgomery Scott: Inquired about fee income sustainability and credit quality. Paracchini expects swap and treasury management fee income to grow, and reported "no material changes" in criticized or classified credit levels.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on loan growth, margin sustainability, capital returns, and commercial payments, with a generally positive but probing tone, seeking clarity on strategy and sustainability of outperformance.

  • Management's tone remained confident and optimistic during both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing flexibility, execution, and growth, with frequent references to "flexibility" and "confidence."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management continues to express high confidence, but analysts have shifted from questions on government shutdown impacts to a stronger focus on capital deployment and emerging business lines.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from caution regarding government shutdowns and regulatory changes to a more assertive stance on crossing $10 billion in assets and maintaining organic growth momentum.

  • Strategic focus evolved from building payments infrastructure to scaling the commercial payments business and targeting higher fee income.

  • Analysts' questions shifted from government SBA program impacts to proactive queries on capital priorities, NII guidance, and noninterest income growth.

  • Key financial metrics such as net interest income, margin, and capital ratios all improved, while credit quality remained stable.

  • Management’s tone remained upbeat, with a greater emphasis on capital flexibility and strategic growth initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty, interest rate volatility, and regulatory changes as challenges but emphasized preparedness and proactive monitoring.

  • Asset sensitivity and the potential impact of Fed rate cuts remain areas of attention, with Bell noting the need to "catch up probably on a gradual kind of declining rate scenario."

  • Loan growth sustainability is tied to commercial activity, with Paracchini stating that real estate growth depends on transaction activity picking up if rates decline.

Final Takeaway

Byline Bancorp’s leadership highlighted strong financial performance and positioned the company for continued growth as it targets crossing the $10 billion asset threshold in 2026. Management underscored its confidence in sustained profitability, organic growth, and capital flexibility, while investing in new business lines and maintaining disciplined credit standards. The company’s approach remains centered on compounding returns, growing commercial payments, and delivering consistent shareholder value through dividends and share repurchases.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Investors should be ‘cautious’ about getting out of this ‘rodeo bull’ market – Morgan Stanley’s Lacamp
data.description:
Jim Lacamp, Morgan Stanley Wealth Management senior vice president, is urging investors to resist the temptation to exit the stock market despite its volatile nature.

During a CNBC interview, Lacamp described the current market environment as a “rodeo bull” due to rapid policy shifts and unpredictable changes but emphasized that the underlying fundamentals remain strong enough to stay invested.

He explained that the whiplash from policy changes—such as tariffs being implemented then reversed—can make it difficult for investors to hold their positions.

“It would be really rare for a market to experience a big bear with interest rates falling, earnings rising, the Fed in a cutting cycle, while stocks are nearing an all-time high,” he said.

Despite the turbulence, he added, “I would caution people about getting out of this market.”

Lacamp pointed to a significant shift in market breadth as evidence of underlying strength. While the “magnificent seven” stocks—Alphabet (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA), and Tesla (TSLA)—dominated returns in recent years as “the only clean shirt in really a dirty laundry basket,” he noted that expansion is now occurring across multiple sectors.

Biotech stocks (XBI), banks (KBE), (KRE), natural resources (PHO), (IGE), (GNR), and small and mid-cap stocks (IJS), (IJR), (IJH), (IWM), are all participating in the rally.

The deregulation environment is particularly benefiting smaller companies, with earnings expectations reflecting this optimism. Lacamp cited projections of 17% earnings growth for mid-caps and 19% for small caps this year, calling it “a big turnaround from what we’ve seen out these areas in the last several years.”

However, Lacamp acknowledged significant risks remain—what he called “flat fat tails on this rodeo bull.”

The administration is attempting to run the economy as hot as possible while still maintaining conditions for the Federal Reserve to continue cutting interest rates, a path he described as “very narrow.”

When asked about potential political disruptions, Lacamp suggested a Supreme Court ruling against existing tariffs would simply be “a buying opportunity.”

“What I am worried about is our ability to control inflation and maintain this rate cutting cycle that we’re in,” he said.

data.title: OceanFirst outlines mid- to high single-digit loan and deposit growth targets for 2026 as Flushing merger advances
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: OceanFirst Financial Corp. (OCFC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Christopher Maher reported "earnings per share of $0.23 on a fully diluted GAAP basis and $0.41 on a core basis," and highlighted "a fifth consecutive quarter of net interest income growth, which increased by $5 million or 5% as compared to the prior quarter and up 14% as compared to the prior-year quarter." Maher emphasized the company's "organic growth momentum" as a direct result of investments made in the first half of 2025 and pointed to a "merger agreement with Flushing Financial Corporation and an investment agreement with Warburg Pincus" as key strategic developments, stating the Flushing acquisition "will directly support our organic growth initiatives in New York, positioning OceanFirst as a scaled competitor in the deepest banking markets in the country."
  • President Joseph Lebel commented that loan originations "totaled just north of $1 billion for the second consecutive quarter and resulted in record quarterly loan growth of $474 million." Lebel noted the C&I business "grew 42% for the year" and pointed to the Premier Bank team's deposit growth of $90 million or 37% from the previous quarter.
  • CFO Patrick Barrett stated, "Pretax pre-provision core earnings grew 9% or $3 million from the prior quarter, driven by earning asset growth over the second half of the year." Barrett added, "Average interest-earning assets increased meaningfully compared to the prior quarter," and confirmed "core noninterest expenses decreased from $72.4 million to $71.2 million, driven by the sale of our title business."

Outlook

  • Barrett stated, "There are no changes to our full year guidance, as stated in the third quarter's earnings release, mid- to high single-digit loan and deposit growth. NII and NIM growing with NIM growing past 3% during the year and NII ramping in the second half of the year. Other income, $7 million to $9 million per quarter and expenses relatively flat to current run rates." He clarified these are standalone expectations and "do not reflect the impact of the Flushing acquisition."
  • Barrett also provided a first quarter outlook: "first quarter core operating expense run rate to remain in the range of $70 million to $71 million, with seasonal compensation increases offset by a full quarter's benefit of our residential outsourcing initiatives."

Financial Results

  • Maher reported "net interest margin of 2.87% declined modestly compared to the third quarter." Total loans for the quarter "increased $474 million, representing an 18% annualized growth rate, driven by $1 billion in originations."
  • Asset quality remained strong with "total loans classified as special mention and substandard decreased 10% to $112 million or just 1% of total loans." Nonperforming loans to total loans were at 0.2% and nonperforming assets to total assets at 0.22%.
  • Core operating expenses of $71 million were "down $1 million or 2% from the linked quarter" due to the outsourcing of the residential lending platform. GAAP operating expenses were $84 million, including $13 million of noncore expenses.
  • Capital levels were described as "robust with an estimated Common Equity Tier 1 capital ratio of 10.7% and tangible book value per share increased to $19.79."

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked for clarification on net interest income guidance and the relationship to loan growth. Barrett replied, "it actually will probably grow at a bit higher clip than whatever our loan balances grow just because of the compounding effect of how big the balance sheet is today." Tamayo also inquired about loan sales post-Flushing merger; Maher responded it is "too early to give you any precise figures on that" but expects to "do some work on the balance sheet in a way that improves our margins and ROA outlook over time while also reducing credit risk."
  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: Asked about C&I growth pace for 2026. Lebel projected "similar growth rates...in the 7% to 9% range." Switzer also asked if the $2 billion to $3 billion deposit target by 2027 for Premier Bank is still valid. Lebel confirmed, "I don't see any reason why we would back off the 2027 targets."
  • Christopher Marinac, Janney Montgomery Scott: Queried about deposit rate trends and Flushing integration. Maher highlighted "a tremendous opportunity" for deposit growth and branch network advantages, while noting the potential for efficiency gains through balance sheet restructuring post-merger.
  • David Bishop, Hovde Group: Asked about geographic drivers of C&I growth and early-stage delinquency uptick. Lebel said growth is "pretty geographically dispersed" and Maher attributed the delinquency to "just one loan" with a federal government lease, not a long-term concern.
  • Matthew Breese, Stephens Inc.: Inquired about loan-to-deposit ratio outlook. Maher expressed a preference to see it "down under 100%" over time but not expecting "dramatically under 100%." Breese also asked about Flushing's deposit mix, to which Maher detailed the distinction between higher-cost and high-quality deposits.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on clarity around loan growth outlook, integration of the Flushing acquisition, and deposit cost trends, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as they sought specifics on growth sustainability and merger benefits.
  • Management maintained a confident and slightly optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, using phrases such as "we're very pleased" and "I don't see any reason why we would back off the 2027 targets," while also noting "too early" to provide some merger-related specifics.
  • Compared to the prior quarter, the tone remained confident with a continued emphasis on disciplined growth and expense management, though Q4 included more forward-looking merger commentary.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Loan originations and growth accelerated compared to Q3, with Q4 reporting record quarterly loan growth of $474 million versus $373 million previously.
  • Deposit growth also increased, with Q4 total deposits up $528 million compared to $203 million in Q3. The Premier Bank team's deposit growth and customer acquisition continued at a strong pace.
  • Asset quality metrics improved further, with special mention and substandard loans declining both in dollar terms and as a percentage of total loans.
  • Operating expenses on a core basis fell, driven by the impact of the residential outsourcing initiative, in contrast to Q3 where restructuring charges were highlighted.
  • Management's tone remained confident, but commentary in Q4 placed more emphasis on the strategic impact and expected benefits of the Flushing merger. Analyst focus shifted toward the integration and future balance sheet optimization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management mentioned the need for further review of the Flushing loan and deposit portfolios post-close, indicating some uncertainty around balance sheet restructuring and ultimate margin impact.
  • Noninterest income decreased due to lower title fees and a reduction in gain on sale of loans linked to outsourcing, with management expecting measured runoff in the residential portfolio.
  • Asset quality concerns were addressed, with Maher explaining a specific uptick in early-stage delinquencies was tied to a single government-related loan, not viewed as a systemic risk.
  • Management cited seasonality and promotional deposit pricing as factors that could influence deposit costs and margin near-term.

Final Takeaway

OceanFirst Financial Corp. closed 2025 with strong loan and deposit growth, effective expense control, and robust asset quality. Management reaffirmed guidance for mid- to high single-digit loan and deposit growth in 2026 and highlighted the anticipated strategic benefits of the Flushing Financial merger, positioning the company for further expansion in key Northeast markets. The company remains focused on organic growth, operational efficiency initiatives, and is prepared to optimize the combined balance sheet as integration progresses.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alaska Air expects 'meaningful' improvement in 2026 EPS
data.description:
Alaska Air Group (ALK) is trending higher and building on Friday’s premarket gains as the company’s remarks during its earnings call on Friday morning helped offset mixed fourth quarter results.

Reflecting better-than-expected capacity ((ASM)) and lower-than-expected costs per available seat mile, ex-fuel ((CASMex)), Alaska Air (ALK) earned an adjusted profit of $0.43 per share, down from $0.97 a year ago, but $0.32 better than Wall Street’s expectations and more than $0.30 better than what the company anticipated.

Revenue of $3.63B increased 3% from a year earlier and resulted in a 0.6% increase in year-over-year revenue per available seat mile ((RASM)) despite the government shutdown but was $10M short of the consensus estimates.   

Premium revenue increased 7%, cargo revenue was up 22%, and loyalty revenue increased 12% from the same quarter last year.

Looking ahead to the first quarter and fiscal year, Alaska Air (ALK) cautions that to hit the higher end of guidance, the macroeconomic recovery will need to be sustained in 2026 and for fuel prices to stabilize.

“Given the inherent uncertainty of the macroeconomic environment, we remain as focused as ever on controlling what is within our control, including disciplined cost management,” the company said in a statement.

During the earnings call Friday morning, CEO Ben Minicucci noted that while 2025 “did not deliver the financial returns we laid out at the start of the year, we strongly delivered against our Alaska Accelerate vision,” adding that 2026 bookings are “very encouraging.”

The three-year Alaska Accelerate initiative aims to generate $1B in incremental profit by expanding long-haul international flights, upgrading its premium offerings, launching a new premium credit card, and investing over $600M to support the combined cargo and fleet operations with a goal of at least 12 widebody international destinations by 2030.

To achieve this, Minicucci said that the company will focus on “harvesting the investments from 2025” and expects full-year earnings to be in the range of $3.50 to $6.50 per share, representing a “meaningful improvement” over 2025.  

Meanwhile, Chief Financial Officer Shane Tackett highlighted the growth in its high-margin premium cabin segment and improvement in its main cabin segment.

“We are excited to see continued growth in our premium cabin revenue and now have 86% of our 218 Boeing 737 seat retrofits complete,” Tackett said on the call, adding that Alaska Air (ALK) has also tapped into the lucrative international corporate travel space.

Accordingly, shares of Alaska Air Group (ALK) pierced resistance at the 200-day moving average with a gain of as much as 6% into the second half of Friday’s trading session.
